event: The Virgin and Child with Saints Elizabeth and John the Baptist
symbols: lamb with the infant John — 'Behold the Lamb of God,' John's future proclamation
nursing infant Christ — the full humanity of the incarnate God
Elizabeth as elderly woman — the miraculous motherhood of the barren woman
the two infants together — the forerunner and the Messiah united from childhood
architectural column — the stability of the Church founded on these relationships
description: A warm, intimate Baroque composition showing four figures. At center, a young woman in a red dress holds a plump nude infant who reaches toward another child below. The infant Christ appears to be nursing or recently nursed. At left, an elderly woman in a dark veil (Elizabeth) smiles warmly and supports the young John the Baptist, a curly-haired toddler who reaches up toward Jesus. A small lamb stands beside John. The setting is before a stone column with a landscape visible at left. Rubens' characteristic warm palette and fleshy, dynamic figures fill the composition.
theological_description: Rubens depicts the meeting of the Holy Family with Elizabeth and the young John the Baptist, building on Luke's account of the Visitation (Luke 1:39-56) and extending it into the childhood of the two boys. The infant John reaching toward Christ prefigures his adult ministry: 'Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!' (John 1:29) — and the lamb beside him makes this symbolism explicit. Elizabeth's joy mirrors her exclamation at the Visitation: 'And why is this granted to me that the mother of my Lord should come to me?' (Luke 1:43). The intimacy between the two families underscores the divine plan connecting the forerunner to the Messiah. Mary nursing Christ emphasizes the Incarnation's radical reality — the Creator of the universe sustained by a woman's milk. The warm, physical affection between the figures expresses Rubens' theology of grace as embodied and relational rather than abstract.

scripture_refs.reason: Elizabeth's baby leaped when Mary arrived — the two families connected
